_comment_double_blind: REVIEW-STAGE METADATA. Information Processing & Management uses double-anonymized peer review, and the submitted manuscript cites this deposit's DOI. Zenodo reads this file when the GitHub release webhook fires, so the creators below MUST stay anonymous until the paper is accepted. The de-anonymized version (named authors, affiliations, corresponding-author email) lives outside this repository in camera_ready_release/.zenodo.json and camera_ready_release/CITATION.cff. On acceptance: replace this file, re-run the release, and edit the Zenodo record's metadata -- the DOI is preserved.
_comment_supersedes: This release SUPERSEDES the version archived at doi:10.5281/zenodo.21305263, whose central conclusions we withdraw. That version's evaluation harness sorted the check-in stream by user rather than by time, so its 15 'streaming rounds' were disjoint user cohorts rather than slices of time: a per-user memory was empty at scoring time for 97% of scored points, and fine-tuning had no adaptation to earn and only forgetting to pay. Both of its headline claims are artifacts of that sort key. See the README and section 6.7 of the paper.
title: Less Is More: Next Point-of-Interest Benchmarks Reward Returning, and a Counter Is Hard to Beat
description: Code, chronological data splits, evaluation protocols and result artifacts reproducing every table and figure in the paper. The benchmarks of this field are shown to be return-prediction benchmarks: 70-76% of the standard evaluation set asks the model to predict a place the user has already visited. A per-user visit counter -- no neural network, no gradients, O(1) per check-in -- beats two states of the art at Acc@10 (official GETNext, SIGIR'22, by 12.6%; official STHGCN, SIGIR'23, by 10.8%), each run by us on that baseline's OWN evaluation instances, while losing to both at Acc@1. Includes: the official baselines and a 7B language model, all run rather than quoted; a chance floor and a retriever ceiling for the LLM protocol; a random-noise control for its fusion gain; and three silent defects with the controls that catch them -- an evaluation sort key (ours), a batch_first mismatch that makes a released transformer attend across the mini-batch instead of across time (theirs), and a float32 accumulator that silently deletes a counting baseline's tie-break (ours, and it corrupted our own audit before we caught it). Three verifier scripts re-derive every number in the manuscript from the artifacts. Author names are withheld pending double-anonymized peer review.
